Integration Standard

passages.heading: 4.1. Three-Layer Internal Architecture
passages.text: Layer A — Professional Knowledge Base. Encoded knowledge of the profession: standards, regulations, methodologies, templates, vocabularies, common patterns. Curated and maintained by professional experts with possible AI assistance for updates .

passages.heading: 4.1. Three-Layer Internal Architecture
passages.text: This is the most expensive layer to build and the most valuable competitive moat. A high-quality professional knowledge base for German social law, for example, represents thousands of hours of expert curation.

passages.heading: 4.1. Three-Layer Internal Architecture
passages.text: Layer B — Generation and Reasoning Engine. The active component: an LLM or ensemble of LLMs configured to operate within Layer A. Receives practitioner input, applies professional knowledge, generates appropriate output.

passages.heading: 4.1. Three-Layer Internal Architecture
passages.text: Layer C — Practitioner Interface and Personalization. The visible application: web/mobile/desktop interface, authentication, personal preferences, work-in-progress storage, integration with practitioner's other tools.

passages.heading: 4.1. Three-Layer Internal Architecture
passages.text: Personalization here does not reach into Layer A — the professional context is shared across all users. Personal preferences UI, language, notification settings, current projects are layered on top.

passages.heading: 4.2. Knowledge Curation Strategies
passages.text: Layer A — the professional knowledge base — is the heart of a Professional Colleague Agent. Several strategies for building and maintaining it:

passages.heading: 4.2. Knowledge Curation Strategies
passages.text: Strategy 1 — Single Expert Curator. A senior professional or small team curates the knowledge base. Highest quality, limited scale. Good for early stages.

passages.heading: 4.2. Knowledge Curation Strategies
passages.text: Strategy 2 — Federation of Practitioners. Many practitioners contribute knowledge fragments through pattern-library mechanism. Quality varies, scale large. Requires curation oversight. This connects directly to the Pattern Library architecture in Nautilus.

passages.heading: 4.2. Knowledge Curation Strategies
passages.text: Strategy 3 — Authority Integration. Direct integration with authoritative sources legal databases, medical guidelines, educational standards . Auto-update as sources update. Most rigorous, requires legal/institutional access.

passages.heading: 4.2. Knowledge Curation Strategies
passages.text: Strategy 4 — Hybrid. Combination of authoritative sources for hard rules and practitioner-contributed patterns for practical applications . Most practical for complex professions.

passages.heading: 4.2. Knowledge Curation Strategies
passages.text: For most domains, Strategy 4 is realistic. The "Обучай" service appears to use this — official ФГОС for hard rules, practitioner-driven refinements for practical application.

passages.heading: 4.3. Update and Versioning
passages.text: Principle of Snapshot Stability. Practitioner work-in-progress is anchored to a specific knowledge base version. Mid-project updates to the knowledge base do not retroactively alter practitioner's drafts. This prevents disorientation.

passages.heading: 4.3. Update and Versioning
passages.text: Principle of Update Awareness. Significant knowledge base updates are surfaced to practitioner. "The relevant statute was amended last week. Your in-progress submission may need review." The practitioner decides whether to update their work.

passages.heading: 4.4. Quality Assurance Architecture
passages.text: Professional outputs have consequences. A flawed legal document, medical record, or educational assessment produces real harm. Quality assurance must be built into the architecture.

passages.heading: 4.4. Quality Assurance Architecture
passages.text: QA Mechanism 1 — Confidence Indicators. The agent reports its confidence in each output element. High confidence: "Standard format applied per current regulation." Low confidence: "Approach uncertain; consult specialist guidance."

passages.heading: 4.4. Quality Assurance Architecture
passages.text: QA Mechanism 2 — Cited Sources. Each substantive claim the agent makes is traceable to a source in the knowledge base. The practitioner can verify by following citations.

passages.heading: 4.4. Quality Assurance Architecture
passages.text: QA Mechanism 3 — Practitioner Review Required. Outputs are drafts requiring practitioner review and acceptance, not final products. The agent's interface should make review natural rather than burdensome.

passages.heading: 4.4. Quality Assurance Architecture
passages.text: QA Mechanism 4 — Error Reporting. Practitioners encountering errors have low-friction way to report them. Aggregated reports inform Layer A improvements.

passages.heading: 4.5. Integration Standards
passages.text: Standard data formats for the profession. Legal documents should output in standard formats typically structured XML for some jurisdictions, formatted PDF/DOCX otherwise . Medical records should output in clinical standards HL7, FHIR . Educational materials in standard exchange formats.

passages.heading: 4.5. Integration Standards
passages.text: Workflow tools the profession uses. Lawyers use specific case management systems. Teachers use specific gradebook systems. Doctors use specific EHR systems. Integration here removes friction.

passages.heading: 4.5. Integration Standards
passages.text: Authority systems the profession is bound to. Legal filings to court systems. Tax submissions to government. Educational certifications. The agent should facilitate authority interactions, not create new parallel ones.

passages.heading: 4.5. Integration Standards
passages.text: This integration work is significant. It is also where many "AI for X profession" projects fail — building a beautiful agent that no practitioner can fit into their existing workflow.

passages.heading: 5. The Economics of Profession-Wide Replication
passages.text: - 5. The Economics of Profession-Wide Replication 5-the-economics-of-profession-wide-replication - 5.1. Cost Structure 51-cost-structure - 5.2. Revenue Models 52-revenue-models - 5.3. Network Effects and Knowledge Compounding 53-network-effects-and-knowledge-compounding - 5.4. Competitive Dynamics 54-competitive-dynamics - 5.5. Sustainability Considerations 55-sustainability-considerations

passages.heading: 5. The Economics of Profession-Wide Replication
passages.text: A defining feature of Professional Colleague Agents is that one agent serves all practitioners of a profession . This has profound economic implications.

passages.heading: 5.1. Cost Structure
passages.text: Layer A knowledge base is high-cost, one-time. Building a high-quality knowledge base for a profession requires substantial expert time. Estimate: 1,000-10,000 expert-hours to bootstrap, ongoing 100-500 hours/year to maintain.

passages.heading: 5.1. Cost Structure
passages.text: Layer B generation engine is low marginal cost. LLM inference costs continue to drop. With current trends, a sophisticated Professional Colleague Agent costs perhaps $5-50 per practitioner per month in LLM API fees, depending on usage intensity.

passages.heading: 5.1. Cost Structure
passages.text: Layer C interface is moderate-cost, one-time. Building a professional-grade interface costs perhaps 2,000-5,000 developer hours. Ongoing maintenance perhaps 500 hours/year.

passages.heading: 5.1. Cost Structure
passages.text: So total fixed costs are high thousands of expert hours but per-user costs are low tens of dollars per month .

passages.heading: 5.2. Revenue Models
passages.text: Model 1 — Practitioner Subscription. Each practitioner pays directly. Common for individual professionals lawyers, accountants, consultants . Requires demonstrating clear ROI to individual practitioner.

passages.heading: 5.2. Revenue Models
passages.text: Model 3 — Government Procurement. State or municipal government purchases for entire profession. Fits well with public professions teachers, social workers, public defenders .

passages.heading: 5.2. Revenue Models
passages.text: Model 4 — Foundation-Funded Public Good. Mission-driven foundation funds development and operation; service provided free or at minimal cost. Particularly fitting for professions serving vulnerable populations.

passages.heading: 5.2. Revenue Models
passages.text: Model 5 — Hybrid. Free tier for basic functions, paid tiers for advanced features. «Обучай» appears to use this model.

passages.heading: 5.2. Revenue Models
passages.text: Model 6 — Cooperative Ownership. Practitioners own the service collectively, paying nominal subscription that funds operations. Most aligned with profession's interests, requires institutional capacity.

passages.heading: 5.2. Revenue Models
passages.text: For OKWF specifically, Model 4 foundation-funded and Model 6 cooperative are most aligned with mission. Model 3 is appropriate for partnerships with public-sector employers.

passages.heading: 5.3. Network Effects and Knowledge Compounding
passages.text: - Common errors → improved Layer A guidance - Effective approaches → patterns added to knowledge base - Edge cases → expanded coverage - Regional variations → specialized sub-knowledge

passages.heading: 5.3. Network Effects and Knowledge Compounding
passages.text: This creates positive feedback. Professional Colleague Agents become more valuable as more practitioners use them. This favors first-movers and platforms with broad reach.

passages.heading: 5.3. Network Effects and Knowledge Compounding
passages.text: For a foundation like OKWF, this means: deploy widely and freely if possible to capture network effects, even at cost of forgoing per-user revenue.

passages.heading: 5.4. Competitive Dynamics
passages.text: - One dominant per profession , due to knowledge compounding - Low switching costs at individual level , but high institutional switching costs - Commoditization of Layer B as LLM providers proliferate - Strong moat at Layer A for established players

passages.heading: 5.5. Sustainability Considerations
passages.text: - Layer A maintenance: ~500 hours/year × $100/hour = $50,000 - LLM costs: ~$30/practitioner/month × 10,000 practitioners × 12 months = $3,600,000 - Interface and infrastructure: ~$200,000/year - Operations: ~$300,000/year

passages.heading: 6. Risks Specific to this Category
passages.text: - 6. Risks Specific to this Category 6-risks-specific-to-this-category - 6.1. Risk: Mediation Collapse 61-risk-mediation-collapse - 6.2. Risk: Skill Atrophy in New Practitioners 62-risk-skill-atrophy-in-new-practitioners - 6.3. Risk: Standardization Lock-In 63-risk-standardization-lock-in - 6.4. Risk: Liability Ambiguity 64-risk-liability-ambiguity - 6.5. Risk: Privacy Cascade 65-risk-privacy-casc

passages.heading: 6.1. Risk: Mediation Collapse
passages.text: Scenario : When both parties to a professional interaction use AI agents, the human content of the interaction approaches zero.

passages.heading: 6.1. Risk: Mediation Collapse
passages.text: Concrete example : Teacher generates assignments with AI. Student completes assignments with AI. Teacher grades with AI. Student receives grade. Where in this chain has actual learning occurred?

passages.heading: 6.1. Risk: Mediation Collapse
passages.text: Mitigation 1 — Asymmetric design. Teacher's agent generates problems requiring active human reasoning, not pattern-matching. Problems for which AI assistance produces poor results. Design assignments AI-resistance into them.

passages.heading: 6.1. Risk: Mediation Collapse
passages.text: Mitigation 2 — Live elements. Some assessments require live human interaction oral exams, project presentations, collaborative work where AI assistance is structurally limited.

passages.heading: 6.1. Risk: Mediation Collapse
passages.text: Mitigation 4 — Reframing of education. If AI changes what students can do, education adapts. The era of homework-as-assessment may end. New forms of assessment emerge.

passages.heading: 6.2. Risk: Skill Atrophy in New Practitioners
passages.text: Scenario : Junior practitioners using the agent from day one never develop foundational skills the agent automates. When agent fails inevitably or is unavailable, practitioner cannot perform basic professional functions.

passages.heading: 6.2. Risk: Skill Atrophy in New Practitioners
passages.text: Concrete example : Junior lawyer uses parajural AI for all routine drafting. Never develops drafting skills personally. Cannot draft when AI is unavailable. Cannot critically review AI output because they don't know what good drafting looks like.

passages.heading: 6.2. Risk: Skill Atrophy in New Practitioners
passages.text: Mitigation 1 — Educational pathway. Professional training includes period of doing work without AI assistance. Like learning to compute by hand before using calculators.

passages.heading: 6.2. Risk: Skill Atrophy in New Practitioners
passages.text: Mitigation 3 — Mandatory review burden. Practitioner must genuinely review agent outputs, not approve mechanically. Spot checks built into workflow.

passages.heading: 6.2. Risk: Skill Atrophy in New Practitioners
passages.text: Mitigation 4 — Acceptable trade-offs. Some skill atrophy is acceptable. We do not lament the loss of mental arithmetic skills due to calculators. The question is which skills are essential to retain.

passages.heading: 6.3. Risk: Standardization Lock-In
passages.text: Scenario : Wide deployment of one agent in a profession homogenizes professional practice. Innovations from outside the agent's training data become harder to introduce. Professional creativity declines.

passages.heading: 6.3. Risk: Standardization Lock-In
passages.text: Concrete example : All teachers use «Обучай»-style assignments. All assignments converge to similar style. Pedagogical innovation slows because non-conventional approaches are harder to access.

passages.heading: 6.3. Risk: Standardization Lock-In
passages.text: Mitigation 3 — Innovation channels. Profession maintains non-AI channels for sharing innovative approaches. Journals, conferences, peer mentorship persist alongside AI-assisted work.

passages.heading: 6.3. Risk: Standardization Lock-In
passages.text: Mitigation 4 — Active diversity. Foundation-funded agents could deliberately offer multiple methodological approaches in their knowledge base, exposing practitioners to variety.

passages.heading: 6.4. Risk: Liability Ambiguity
passages.text: Scenario : Practitioner uses agent-generated output that turns out to contain professional errors. Who is liable? Practitioner who used? Agent provider? Knowledge base curator? LLM provider underlying generation?

passages.heading: 6.4. Risk: Liability Ambiguity
passages.text: Concrete example : Lawyer files brief with citation generated by parajural AI. Citation turns out to be hallucination. Court sanctions lawyer. Was the lawyer negligent? Was the AI provider?

passages.heading: 6.4. Risk: Liability Ambiguity
passages.text: Mitigation 1 — Explicit practitioner responsibility. Service terms make clear: practitioner is responsible for outputs they use. Agent is tool, not professional.

passages.heading: 6.4. Risk: Liability Ambiguity
passages.text: Mitigation 4 — Transparent error rates. Agent providers publish error rates and known failure modes. Practitioners factor these into use.

passages.heading: 6.5. Risk: Privacy Cascade
passages.text: Scenario : Practitioner uses agent on case involving sensitive client data. Sensitive data flows through agent infrastructure. Breach exposes client data.

passages.heading: 6.5. Risk: Privacy Cascade
passages.text: Concrete example : Therapist uses Professional Colleague Agent for note synthesis. Patient privacy is compromised when agent provider has security incident.

passages.heading: 6.5. Risk: Privacy Cascade
passages.text: Mitigation 1 — Local-first deployment. For high-sensitivity domains, agent runs entirely on practitioner's device or institutional infrastructure. No cloud dependencies.

passages.heading: 6.5. Risk: Privacy Cascade
passages.text: Mitigation 2 — Anonymization at input. Practitioner uses anonymized representations when working with agent. Real identity stays in practitioner's separate records.

passages.heading: 6.6. Risk: Subtle Bias in Knowledge Base
passages.text: Scenario : Layer A knowledge base reflects biases of its curators. Practitioners using agent inherit these biases. Bias spreads systematically.

passages.heading: 6.6. Risk: Subtle Bias in Knowledge Base
passages.text: Concrete example : Legal Professional Colleague Agent's knowledge base over-represents perspectives of one school of legal thought. Practitioners using agent draft submissions subtly aligned with that school. Legal pluralism declines.

passages.heading: 6.7. Risk: Profession Capture by Service Providers
passages.text: Concrete example : Single AI provider serves 90% of teachers. Provider's design choices effectively determine pedagogy nationally. Provider's pricing and terms determine teacher working conditions.

passages.heading: 6.7. Risk: Profession Capture by Service Providers
passages.text: This risk is real and serious. «Обучай» growing to 93,000 teachers in 7 months illustrates speed at which capture can emerge.

passages.heading: 7. Application Domains
passages.text: - 7. Application Domains 7-application-domains - 7.1. K-12 Education 71-k-12-education - 7.2. Legal Practice Sub-Specialties 72-legal-practice-sub-specialties - 7.3. Medical Practice Specific Specialties 73-medical-practice-specific-specialties - 7.4. Social Work and Case Management 74-social-work-and-case-management - 7.5. Mental Health Care 75-mental-health-care - 7.6. Architecture and Engineeri

passages.heading: 7. Application Domains
passages.text: We identify nine professions or sub-professions where Professional Colleague Agents are particularly applicable. Order is by readiness — most ready first.

passages.heading: 7.2. Legal Practice (Sub-Specialties)
passages.text: Notable opportunity : German social law / disability rights SGB IX, XII . Underserved by current commercial offerings. Author's domain. See Section 8.

passages.heading: 7.8. Government Service Delivery
passages.text: Caveat : This domain is particularly susceptible to several risks — mediation collapse, bias, profession capture. Especially careful design needed.

passages.heading: 8. Pilot Proposal: SGB Advocate Colleague
passages.text: - 8. Pilot Proposal: SGB Advocate Colleague 8-pilot-proposal-sgb-advocate-colleague - 8.1. Context 81-context - 8.2. Target Practitioners 82-target-practitioners - 8.3. Burden Addressed 83-burden-addressed - 8.4. Knowledge Base Requirements 84-knowledge-base-requirements - 8.5. Pilot Cohort 85-pilot-cohort - 8.6. Resource Requirements 86-resource-requirements - 8.7. Expected Outcomes 87-expected-o

passages.heading: 8. Pilot Proposal: SGB Advocate Colleague
passages.text: We now apply the Professional Colleague Agent framework to a specific opportunity: an agent for advocates in German social law SGB , particularly disability and welfare rights.

passages.heading: 8.1. Context
passages.text: The author of this paper engages directly with German social law through ongoing Sozialgericht proceedings cases S 6 SO 58/26 ER and S 7 SO 99/25 in Dresden . Direct experience shows that legal advocacy in this domain is severely under-resourced. Many qualified disabled people lose entitlements through procedural failures because they cannot access adequate representation.

passages.heading: 8.1. Context
passages.text: This is the same domain identified in Representative Agent Layer paper as Domain 4 vulnerable citizens navigating bureaucracy . Professional Colleague Agent for advocates serving this population could amplify their capacity substantially.

passages.heading: 8.3. Burden Addressed
passages.text: Routine drafting : - Widerspruch against rejected applications - Klage lawsuit submission to Sozialgericht - Stellungnahme on counterparty filings - Antrag for procedural relief - Eilantrag for emergency proceedings

passages.heading: 8.3. Burden Addressed
passages.text: Knowledge lookup : - Relevant SGB statutes IX, XII primarily - BSG decisions and precedent e.g., B 8 SO 9/19 R - Local Sozialgericht procedural variations - Federal-state law interaction

passages.heading: 8.3. Burden Addressed
passages.text: Procedural management : - Deadline tracking Fristwahrung - Document organization across multiple proceedings - Evidence inventory and citation - Hearing preparation

passages.heading: 8.4. Knowledge Base Requirements
passages.text: Statutory knowledge : - Full SGB IX rehabilitation, disability - Full SGB XII social assistance - Relevant SGG procedural law - Selected SGB I, II, III, V, XI cross-references

passages.heading: 8.4. Knowledge Base Requirements
passages.text: Procedural standards : - Sozialgericht procedural conventions - Local court practices Dresden specifically for pilot - Standard formatting for filings

passages.heading: 8.4. Knowledge Base Requirements
passages.text: Pattern library : - Successful Widerspruch templates by issue type - Standard arguments for common rejection patterns - Anonymized successful approaches

passages.heading: 8.4. Knowledge Base Requirements
passages.text: Bureaucratic knowledge : - KSV Sachsen practices and known patterns - Sozialamt Dresden practices - Common procedural errors by authorities

passages.heading: 8.5. Pilot Cohort
passages.text: Phase 0 Months 1-6 : Bootstrap with 5-10 advocates, including the author, plus 1-2 lawyers willing to test intensively. Build initial Layer A. Refine through real use.

passages.heading: 8.6. Resource Requirements
passages.text: Initial development Year 1 : - 1 senior legal expert curator full-time: €100,000 - 2 software engineers: €200,000 - LLM API costs: €30,000 - Infrastructure: €20,000 - Legal compliance: €30,000 - Operations: €50,000 - Total Year 1: €430,000

passages.heading: 8.6. Resource Requirements
passages.text: Steady state Year 2+ : - Curation maintenance 0.5 FTE : €60,000 - Engineering maintenance 1 FTE : €100,000 - LLM API costs scaled : €100,000-300,000 - Infrastructure: €30,000 - Operations: €50,000 - Total annual: €340,000-540,000

passages.heading: 8.6. Resource Requirements
passages.text: For 500-1,000 active practitioners, this is roughly €500-1,000 per practitioner per year — viable for foundation funding or modest practitioner subscriptions.

passages.heading: 8.7. Expected Outcomes
passages.text: For practitioners : - 30-50% time reduction on routine drafting - Improved consistency in formal compliance - Faster ramp-up for new advocates

passages.heading: 8.7. Expected Outcomes
passages.text: For knowledge ecosystem : - Public pattern library of successful approaches - Improved baseline for self-representation - Anonymized case insights for academic study

passages.heading: 8.7. Expected Outcomes
passages.text: For broader project : - Reference implementation for OKWF Professional Colleague Agents - Demonstration of foundation-funded model - Connection to Representative Agent Layer advocates use agents for clients who could later use Representative Agents directly

passages.heading: 8.8. Why This Domain First
passages.text: This pilot provides the OKWF concept its first concrete deployment. From here, expansion to adjacent domains becomes incremental rather than novel.

passages.heading: 10.1. Serialization
passages.text: - to json — для MCP / API - to markdown — для CLI и LLM consumption - to html — для web interface

passages.heading: 9. Relationship to Other Agent Types
passages.text: - 9. Relationship to Other Agent Types 9-relationship-to-other-agent-types - 9.1. With Personal AI Assistants Type 0 91-with-personal-ai-assistants-type-0 - 9.2. With Institutional Agents Type 2 92-with-institutional-agents-type-2 - 9.3. With Employee Agents Type 3 93-with-employee-agents-type-3 - 9.4. With Representative Agents Type 4 94-with-representative-agents-type-4 - 9.5. Stack-Level View 9

passages.heading: 9.1. With Personal AI Assistants (Type 0)
passages.text: Relationship : Complementary. Personal AI Assistants handle general tasks writing emails, scheduling, brainstorming . Professional Colleague Agents handle profession-specific tasks.

passages.heading: 9.1. With Personal AI Assistants (Type 0)
passages.text: Integration : A practitioner uses both. ChatGPT for general writing assistance. Professional Colleague Agent for profession-specific work. Different tools for different contexts.

passages.heading: 9.1. With Personal AI Assistants (Type 0)
passages.text: Risk : Confusion about when to use which. Mitigated by clear positioning: Professional Colleague Agent has the professional context; Personal AI Assistant does not.

passages.heading: 9.2. With Institutional Agents (Type 2)
passages.text: Relationship : Professional Colleague Agent typically feeds outputs into Institutional Agent. Teacher generates lesson plan with Professional Colleague Type 1 , then exports to school's grading system Type 2 .

passages.heading: 9.2. With Institutional Agents (Type 2)
passages.text: Integration challenge : Layer C interface layer of Type 1 needs to talk to Type 2 systems. This is significant engineering work and varies by institution.

passages.heading: 9.3. With Employee Agents (Type 3)
passages.text: Relationship : Practitioner may delegate specific zones of authority to Employee Agents while using Professional Colleague for direct work. Teacher uses Professional Colleague for lesson plan generation; Employee Agent handles routine parent emails autonomously.

passages.heading: 9.3. With Employee Agents (Type 3)
passages.text: Boundary issue : When does helping Type 1 become acting Type 3 ? Lawyer using agent for drafting is Type 1. Lawyer authorizing agent to file routine motions automatically would be Type 3.

passages.heading: 9.3. With Employee Agents (Type 3)
passages.text: The line is consequential. Type 1 has minimal regulatory concerns. Type 3 raises substantial questions about professional licensure and authority.

passages.heading: 9.3. With Employee Agents (Type 3)
passages.text: Practical recommendation : Maintain clear distinction. Type 1 first; Type 3 carefully and only after professional and regulatory frameworks adapt.

passages.heading: 9.4. With Representative Agents (Type 4)
passages.text: Relationship : Different functions, complementary deployment. Professional Colleague serves practitioner's daily work. Representative Agent serves practitioner's career and market position.

passages.heading: 9.4. With Representative Agents (Type 4)
passages.text: Sequencing : Professional Colleague Agents are higher-readiness for deployment. They should precede Representative Agent deployment in any practitioner's adoption journey. By the time practitioners are comfortable with Type 1, they are better prepared to evaluate Type 4.

passages.heading: 9.4. With Representative Agents (Type 4)
passages.text: For OKWF specifically : Deploy Professional Colleague Agents in Phase 1. Add Representative Agent Layer in Phase 2-3, after practitioners and infrastructure are mature.

passages.heading: 9.4. With Representative Agents (Type 4)
passages.text: This sequencing differs from what Representative Agent Layer paper proposed. Reflection on the «Обучай» case suggests Type 1 is better starting point — both because it's more deployable and because it builds the trust base necessary for Type 4.

passages.heading: 9.5. Stack-Level View
passages.text: Each is built differently. Each has different stakeholders. Each requires different governance. Conflating them produces confusion. Distinguishing them enables clear architecture.

passages.heading: 10.1. Scope of "Profession"
passages.text: What constitutes a profession for the purposes of agent specialization? Can sub-specialties have their own agents family law vs. criminal law vs. social law ? What's the right granularity?

passages.heading: 10.1. Scope of "Profession"
passages.text: Answer likely emerges from market: where practitioners cluster and where shared knowledge bases provide enough overlap to justify development cost.

passages.heading: 10.2. Multi-Profession Practitioners
passages.text: How do agents handle practitioners who span multiple professional contexts researcher who also teaches; lawyer who is also academic; engineer who is also entrepreneur ?

passages.heading: 10.3. International Variation
passages.text: A Professional Colleague Agent for German social law cannot serve American social law practitioners. How do we structure international variation? Per-country agents? Modular knowledge bases with country-specific layers?

passages.heading: 10.4. Update Cadence
passages.text: How quickly should knowledge bases update as authoritative sources change? Real-time? Daily? Quarterly? With release cadences? Different for different types of changes emergency vs. ordinary ?

passages.heading: 10.5. Quality Assessment
passages.text: How do we measure quality of a Professional Colleague Agent? Practitioner satisfaction? Time savings? Output accuracy measured how ? Long-term professional development? Different metrics serve different stakeholders.

passages.heading: 10.6. Transition Costs
passages.text: When practitioners move between Professional Colleague Agents switching providers , what's the friction? Personal preferences and history transfer? Trained habits? Modified knowledge bases that practitioner has customized?

passages.heading: 10.7. Profession-Wide Effects
passages.text: Beyond individual practitioner effects, what happens to a profession as a whole when most practitioners use the same Professional Colleague Agent? Does practice homogenize? Does quality improve? Does innovation slow? Empirical question requiring measurement.

passages.heading: 10.8. Appropriate Resistance
passages.text: Should some professions resist Professional Colleague Agents? Are there professional values that AI augmentation undermines? What might we lose? These are not technical questions but they shape technical decisions.

passages.heading: 11.1. For Professional Practitioners
passages.text: Especially in social law, education, healthcare, social work : Tell us what burdens you most. What routine work absorbs your time? What would you delegate to an AI colleague? What would you never delegate? Pilot participation invited.

passages.heading: 11.2. For Domain Experts (Curators)
passages.text: Layer A construction requires experts willing to encode professional knowledge into structured form. This is detailed, careful work. We need curators willing to invest time — preferably compensated.

passages.heading: 11.3. For Software Engineers
passages.text: Build Layer B and C. Open-source contributions to reference implementations welcome. Particularly engineers with experience in: - LLM application architecture - Multi-language support - Accessibility - Privacy-preserving computation

passages.heading: 11.4. For Foundation Funders
passages.text: Foundation-funded Professional Colleague Agents are a high-leverage mission opportunity. €0.5M-3M can deploy agent for a profession of 10,000-100,000 practitioners. Population impact through practitioner amplification is substantial.

passages.heading: 11.5. For Existing Professional Tool Vendors
passages.text: Many existing tool vendors are evolving toward Professional Colleague Agent capability. Open standards collaboration accelerates field. Collaboration on Layer A standards particularly valuable.

passages.heading: 11.6. For Researchers
passages.text: Empirical studies of Professional Colleague Agent deployment needed. Effects on practice quality, profession evolution, practitioner satisfaction, client outcomes. Several years of deployment data now becoming available «Обучай», early medical AI deployments .

passages.heading: 11.7. For Policy Stakeholders
passages.text: Regulatory frameworks for Professional Colleague Agents are nascent. Liability, professional licensure interaction, quality standards — all need development. Engagement with practitioners during framework development valuable.

passages.heading: 12. Closing
passages.text: The Representative Agent Layer paper, when first written, described AI agents on the principal side as a single new category. Reflection — particularly through the empirical case of «Обучай» and the architectural distinctions it makes visible — reveals this was an oversimplification.

passages.heading: 12. Closing
passages.text: Of these, Professional Colleague Agents are the most deployable today . They build on existing professional infrastructure, augment rather than replace, externalize no decisions, replicate efficiently across professions, and address burdens practitioners themselves recognize.

passages.heading: 12. Closing
passages.text: This makes them the natural first production deployment for foundation-funded AI infrastructure. Not because they are most ambitious — Representative Agents are more ambitious — but because they are most ready.

passages.heading: 12. Closing
passages.text: Within the Open Knowledge Work Foundation strategy, this suggests revising the rollout plan: Professional Colleague Agents first, Representative Agents in subsequent phases. Specifically, an SGB Advocate Colleague pilot can demonstrate the model and serve real need simultaneously.

passages.heading: 12. Closing
passages.text: The other types — Personal AI Assistants, Institutional Agents, Employee Agents — also have their roles. A mature deployment for any individual practitioner involves multiple types operating together. But Professional Colleague Agents are where to begin.

passages.heading: 12. Closing
passages.text: «Обучай»'s growth from zero to 93,000 teachers in seven months suggests these systems, when well-designed and addressing real burdens, achieve adoption rapidly. The question is not whether Professional Colleague Agents will proliferate — they will. The question is whether they proliferate within frameworks of foundation governance, practitioner ownership, professional standards, and ethical care,

passages.heading: 12. Closing
passages.text: The choice is, again, institutional rather than technological. We propose the more careful path. We invite collaboration in walking it.

passages.heading: Acknowledgments
passages.text: This paper emerged through dialogue with Claude Anthropic on April 26, 2026, prompted by author's review of the Russian-language interview with Konstantin Chukavin in Fontanka.ru describing «Обучай». The fivefold typology of principal-side agents emerged from the user's observation that "Representative Agent" was an insufficient category to capture the full landscape.

passages.heading: Acknowledgments
passages.text: Konstantin Chukavin's «Обучай» service serves as central empirical reference. Public information from the Fontanka interview informs case study. No formal partnership with or endorsement of «Обучай» exists; it is documented as publicly observable case.

passages.heading: Appendix A: Comparative Table — Five Agent Types
passages.text: Property Type 0: Personal AI Assistant Type 1: Professional Colleague Type 2: Institutional Type 3: Employee Type 4: Representative ---------- --- --- --- --- --- Specialization None general Single profession Profession + institution Specific task Specific individual Direction Reactive Reactive within profession Bidirectional Autonomous in scope Outward to world External communication None None So

passages.heading: Appendix B: Decision Framework — When to Build Type 1 First
passages.text: Choose Type 1 Professional Colleague Agent when: - Profession has well-defined standardized practices - Practitioners face significant routine burden - Professional context is encodable in knowledge base - No strong external communication requirement - Regulatory environment for profession is clearer than for AI

passages.heading: Appendix B: Decision Framework — When to Build Type 1 First
passages.text: Choose Type 0 Personal AI Assistant when: - Use case is general-purpose, not profession-specific - User has no specific professional context to embed - Mass-market consumer product

passages.heading: Appendix B: Decision Framework — When to Build Type 1 First
passages.text: Choose Type 2 Institutional Agent when: - Institution has specific data/process integration needs - Institutional rules are central to value delivery - Per-institution customization is expected and accepted

passages.heading: Appendix B: Decision Framework — When to Build Type 1 First
passages.text: Choose Type 3 Employee Agent when: - Routine task can be cleanly delegated - Liability framework permits autonomous action - Quality of routine outputs is verifiable

passages.heading: Appendix B: Decision Framework — When to Build Type 1 First
passages.text: Choose Type 4 Representative Agent when: - Individual users need market navigation - Personal context is rich and unique - Long-term career-arc representation is valuable - Trust infrastructure is mature

passages.heading: Appendix C: Quick-Start Architecture for SGB Advocate Colleague
passages.text: Stack suggested : - Backend: Python FastAPI or Go - LLM: Claude Anthropic primary; Qwen 2.5 local for privacy-sensitive operations - Storage: PostgreSQL structured + ChromaDB vector - Knowledge base format: Markdown files in Git repository - Frontend: TypeScript + React - Mobile: React Native or PWA

passages.heading: Appendix C: Quick-Start Architecture for SGB Advocate Colleague
passages.text: Generation pattern for Widerspruch : INPUT: - Bescheid text decoded by agent - Case context practitioner-supplied - Affected statute references extracted by agent

passages.heading: Appendix C: Quick-Start Architecture for SGB Advocate Colleague
passages.text: PROCESS: 1. Extract Bescheid claims and reasoning 2. Identify legal grounds for opposition 3. Locate relevant precedent 4. Check for procedural errors 5. Apply local court conventions 6. Generate draft using template + customization 7. Provide citations for all substantive claims 8. Flag uncertainty where confidence low

passages.heading: Appendix C: Quick-Start Architecture for SGB Advocate Colleague
passages.text: OUTPUT: - Draft Widerspruch DOCX format - Citations document - Risk/uncertainty notes - Suggested additional research - Deadline calendar entry

passages.heading: Appendix C: Quick-Start Architecture for SGB Advocate Colleague
passages.text: Privacy architecture : - Practitioner notes stored encrypted, accessible only to practitioner - LLM API calls with anonymized representations where possible - Optional local-only mode for highest sensitivity - All processing logs auditable by practitioner

passages.heading: Аннотация
passages.text: Развивая статью «Слой Представительских Агентов», мы замечаем, что «AI-агенты на стороне принципала» — не одна категория, а структурированная семья из как минимум пяти различных типов: Персональные AI-помощники, Профессиональные Коллеги-Агенты, Институциональные Агенты, Агенты-Сотрудники и Представительские Агенты. Каждый тип служит разной потребности, имеет разную экономику, требует разного управ

passages.heading: Аннотация
passages.text: Эта статья детально разрабатывает категорию Профессионального Коллеги-Агента. Мы утверждаем, что Профессиональные Коллеги-Агенты — это точка входа с наименьшим трением и наибольшим рычагом для развёртывания AI в квалифицированных профессиях, потому что они а работают в уже стандартизованных профессиональных контекстах, б дополняют, а не заменяют человека-профессионала, в не делегируют никаких реше

passages.heading: Аннотация
passages.text: Мы документируем эмпирический кейс из российского образования сервис «Обучай», который вырос с нуля до 93 000 учителей-пользователей за семь месяцев в 2025-2026 годах , разрабатываем формальную архитектуру и принципы дизайна, выявляем риски, специфичные для этой категории, и предлагаем стратегию развёртывания, конкретно применённую к немецкой социально-правовой области, релевантной работе автора и

passages.heading: Аннотация
passages.text: Мы утверждаем, что Профессиональные Коллеги-Агенты должны быть первым продакшн-развёртыванием AI-инфраструктуры, финансируемой фондом, с Представительскими Агентами как расширением второй фазы, после того как профессионалы интегрируют AI-партнёров в свою ежедневную работу.

Тип 2 — Институциональные Агенты 13-тип-2-институциональные-агенты - 1.4. Тип 3 — Агенты-Сотрудники 14-тип-3-агент

passages.heading: 1. Типология из пяти типов агентов на стороне принципала
passages.text: Статья «Слой Представительских Агентов» представила один тип AI-агента на стороне принципала. Тем самым она неявно предположила, что «AI-агент, обслуживающий человека» — это единственная категория. Эта формулировка, при размышлении, скрывает важные различия. Теперь мы выделяем пять различных типов.

passages.heading: 1.2. Тип 1 — Профессиональные Коллеги-Агенты
passages.text: Функция : Помощник, специализированный для конкретной профессии. Встраивает профессиональные знания нормы, методологии, регуляции, шаблоны , чтобы практикующим не приходилось заново объяснять свой контекст.

passages.heading: 1.4. Тип 3 — Агенты-Сотрудники
passages.text: Примеры : AI, обрабатывающий рутинную почту автономно. AI, проводящий начальные экзамены студентов. AI, обрабатывающий стандартные страховые претензии. AI, делающий первичный скрининг при найме.

passages.heading: 1.6. Слоистая архитектура на стороне принципала
passages.text: Эта типология не иерархия способностей. Каждый тип служит разной функции. Тип 1 не «менее продвинут, чем» Тип 4; он «отличается от». Зрелое развёртывание AI-инфраструктуры для профессионалов, вероятно, включает несколько типов, работающих вместе.

passages.heading: 1.7. Почему это различение важно
passages.text: Свойство Тип 0 Тип 1 Тип 2 Тип 3 Тип 4 ---------- ------- ------- ------- ------- ------- Специализация Нет Профессия Институция+профессия Задача Личность Внешние коммуникации Нет Нет Некоторые Некоторые Обширные Тиражируемость Универсальная По профессии По институции По задаче По индивиду Экономика Подписка По профессии Институциональная Различная Индивидуальная Этические вопросы Низкие Средние С

passages.heading: 2. Что делает агента Профессиональным Коллегой
passages.text: - 2. Что делает агента Профессиональным Коллегой 2-что-делает-агента-профессиональным-коллегой - 2.1. Определяющие свойства 21-определяющие-свойства - 2.2. Что делает Профессиональный Коллега-Агент 22-что-делает-профессиональный-коллега-агент - 2.3. Что Профессиональный Коллега-Агент НЕ делает 23-что-профессиональный-коллега-агент-не-делает - 2.4. Метафора «Коллеги» 24-метафора-коллеги

passages.heading: 2.1. Определяющие свойства
passages.text: Профессиональный Коллега-Агент имеет шесть определяющих свойств. Вместе они составляют тип; при отсутствии любого из них система попадает в другую категорию.

passages.heading: 2.1. Определяющие свойства
passages.text: Свойство 1 — Встроенный профессиональный контекст. Агент имеет глубокое, постоянное знание профессиональных норм, методологий, регуляций, словаря и шаблонов. Практикующему не нужно заново объяснять профессиональный контекст при каждом взаимодействии.

passages.heading: 2.1. Определяющие свойства
passages.text: Свойство 2 — Специализация на единственной профессии. Агент обслуживает одну определённую профессию или один определённый подвид . Не «несколько профессий». Не «общая профессиональная работа». Этот фокус обеспечивает глубину.

passages.heading: 2.1. Определяющие свойства
passages.text: Свойство 3 — Дополнение, не замена. Агент помогает человеку-практикующему делать его работу лучше. Практикующий остаётся ответственным за результаты. Агент не производит окончательных профессиональных результатов в одиночку.

passages.heading: 2.1. Определяющие свойства
passages.text: Свойство 4 — Никаких внешних коммуникаций. Агент не общается со сторонами за пределами практикующего. Результаты предоставляются практикующему для проверки и использования. Это отличает от Представительских Агентов и частично от Агентов-Сотрудников.

passages.heading: 2.1. Определяющие свойства
passages.text: Свойство 5 — Массовое тиражирование. Один и тот же дизайн агента обслуживает всех практикующих профессии. Кастомизация под конкретного пользователя ограничена пользовательскими предпочтениями и работой-в-процессе. Сам профессиональный контекст является общим.

passages.heading: 2.1. Определяющие свойства
passages.text: Свойство 6 — Признание нагрузки. Агент адресует работу, которую сами практикующие признают обременительной или малоценной. Это существенно для принятия — агенты, которые «помогают» с работой, которую практикующие любят, будут отвергнуты.

passages.heading: 2.2. Что делает Профессиональный Коллега-Агент
passages.text: Функция 1 — Генерация в профессиональном формате. Создавать черновики, материалы, планы, анализы в форматах, специфичных для профессии. Учительский агент генерирует планы уроков в образовательных форматах. Юридический агент генерирует обращения в юридических форматах. Медицинский агент генерирует клинические заметки в медицинских форматах. Формат имеет значение, потому что профессиональная коммуни

passages.heading: 2.2. Что делает Профессиональный Коллега-Агент
passages.text: Функция 2 — Применение профессиональных стандартов. Применять соответствующие стандарты, регуляции, методологии автоматически. Учителю не нужно вручную консультироваться с образовательными стандартами каждый раз. Юристу не нужно искать соответствующие статуты. Врачу не нужно помнить клинические рекомендации. Агент применяет это в фоне.

passages.heading: 2.2. Что делает Профессиональный Коллега-Агент
passages.text: Функция 3 — Автоматизация рутинных задач. Заниматься повторяющимися компонентами профессиональной работы, которые потребляют непропорционально много времени относительно ценности. Ввод оценок. Заполнение форм. Стандартная корреспонденция. Документационные рутины.

passages.heading: 2.2. Что делает Профессиональный Коллега-Агент
passages.text: Функция 4 — Проверка качества. Выявлять вероятные ошибки, упущения, несоответствия в черновых результатах практикующего. Действует как осведомлённый коллега, проверяющий работу.

passages.heading: 2.2. Что делает Профессиональный Коллега-Агент
passages.text: Функция 5 — Поиск знаний. Обеспечивать быстрый доступ к профессиональным базам знаний — прецеденты, клиническая литература, методологические ссылки, обновления регуляций. Сокращает время исследований.

passages.heading: 2.3. Что Профессиональный Коллега-Агент НЕ делает
passages.text: Не внешние коммуникации. Не связывается с пациентами, студентами, клиентами или контрагентами от имени практикующего. Все такие контакты остаются за человеком.

passages.heading: 2.3. Что Профессиональный Коллега-Агент НЕ делает
passages.text: Эти границы критичны. Они сохраняют профессиональную действующую силу, разумно распределяют ответственность и избегают расширения сферы в этически более сложную территорию.

passages.heading: 2.4. Метафора «Коллеги»
passages.text: «Коллега» предполагает равного в профессии, общий контекст, взаимное уважение, параллельную работу. Коллега-врач, проверяющий ваш случай. Старший учитель, дающий совет. Коллеги привносят профессиональную экспертизу в отношения.

passages.heading: 2.4. Метафора «Коллеги»
passages.text: Профессиональный Коллега-Агент занимает третью позицию. Не пассивный инструмент. Не подчинённый помощник. Участник профессионального контекста на уровне коллег, который случайно оказывается AI.
