passages.text: - 1. Why the Binary View Is Incomplete 1-why-the-binary-view-is-incomplete - 1.1. The Problem with the Existing Taxonomy 11-the-problem-with-the-existing-taxonomy - 1.2. How Mastery Actually Develops 12-how-mastery-actually-develops - 1.3. The Architecture That Should Match 13-the-architecture-that-should-match - 1.4. Why This Was Not Recognized Earlier 14-why-this-was-not-recognized-earlier

passages.heading: 1.1. The Problem with the Existing Taxonomy
passages.text: Professional Colleague Agents PCA v1.0 introduced five types of principal-side agents. Of these, Type 1 Professional Colleague Agent and Type 4 Representative Agent anchor the extremes of specialization:

passages.heading: 1.1. The Problem with the Existing Taxonomy
passages.text: Type 1 : One agent design serves all practitioners of a profession. The teaching agent serves all teachers. The legal agent for social law serves all social law lawyers.

passages.heading: 1.1. The Problem with the Existing Taxonomy
passages.text: The taxonomy treats these as endpoints of a spectrum but does not develop the spectrum itself. The implicit suggestion is that practitioners either need profession-wide standardization Type 1 or individual-unique representation Type 4 .

passages.heading: 1.2. How Mastery Actually Develops
passages.text: They share a profession. A musician is a musician. A lawyer is a lawyer. The profession provides a shared foundation, common vocabulary, baseline competencies, recognized credentials.

passages.heading: 1.2. How Mastery Actually Develops
passages.text: They are differentiated within the profession. No two musicians are identical. No two lawyers practice exactly the same way. Each combines particular specializations, particular influences, particular emphases that make their work distinctive.

passages.heading: 1.2. How Mastery Actually Develops
passages.text: This second characteristic is not exceptional but typical. It is how professions actually contain their members . The profession provides the genus; specialization provides the species; combination provides the individual instance.

passages.heading: 1.2. How Mastery Actually Develops
passages.text: Importantly, the differentiation usually comes not from inventing entirely novel skills but from specific combinations of generally available skills . A jazz pianist who also studied classical composition and has expertise in audio engineering is differentiated not because any of those skills is unique to her, but because the combination is rare.

passages.heading: 1.3. The Architecture That Should Match
passages.text: A Type 1 agent one shape fits all teachers leaves out the specialization. A Type 4 agent uniquely built for one teacher duplicates work that could be shared.

passages.heading: 1.3. The Architecture That Should Match
passages.text: What matches is an architecture where: - Many narrow-specialist sub-agents exist, each built once - Sub-agents are shared across all practitioners who need that specialization - Each principal configures their own combination of relevant sub-agents - A coordinating layer presents this combination as a coherent composite agent

passages.heading: 1.4. Why This Was Not Recognized Earlier
passages.text: The Composite Skills Agent pattern emerges naturally once the question is asked: "What about practitioners whose distinctive value comes from combining specializations?" But this question was not asked in earlier papers because the framing was profession-centric Type 1 or individual-centric Type 4 without explicit attention to the most common middle ground.

passages.heading: 1.4. Why This Was Not Recognized Earlier
passages.text: This mirrors a common pattern in technology architecture: the extremes get attention because they are conceptually clean; the middle gets neglected because it is messier. Yet the middle is where most actual practice happens.

passages.heading: 1.4. Why This Was Not Recognized Earlier
passages.text: Recognizing the Composite Skills Agent pattern is not a minor extension. It changes the practical strategy for deploying AI support to skilled professions.

passages.heading: 2. The Twenty-One Teachers Pattern
passages.text: - 2. The Twenty-One Teachers Pattern 2-the-twenty-one-teachers-pattern - 2.1. The Story 21-the-story - 2.2. What This Pattern Reveals 22-what-this-pattern-reveals - 2.3. Why This Pattern Generalizes 23-why-this-pattern-generalizes - 2.4. AI Architecture Should Mirror This 24-ai-architecture-should-mirror-this

passages.heading: 2.1. The Story
passages.text: A journalist interviewing an Indian yoga master asked how he became a teacher. The master replied that before he became a teacher in his own right, he had studied with twenty different teachers. Each was a narrow specialist:

passages.heading: 2.1. The Story
passages.text: - One taught a particular asana lineage - Another, specific pranayama techniques - Another, the philosophical foundations - Another, therapeutic applications for back pain - Another, work with mental health conditions - Another, the relationship between hatha yoga and physiotherapy - Another, meditation in the Patanjali tradition - Another, breath work for performers - Another, teaching children -

passages.heading: 2.1. The Story
passages.text: The master, asked to characterize his own teaching, said: none of his twenty teachers taught what he teaches . Each contributed a specialization. The combination — that twenty-first teacher — was uniquely his. He had selected from a pool of hundreds of possible teachers across decades, choosing the twenty whose combined teachings formed his particular path.

passages.heading: 2.2. What This Pattern Reveals
passages.text: The teachers are individually general. Each of the twenty teaches their specialty to many students. Any of them might be chosen by a different student building a different combination.

passages.heading: 2.2. What This Pattern Reveals
passages.text: The combination is individually uncommon. While each teacher is general, the specific selection of twenty out of hundreds available and the way those twenty combine in this particular student's understanding is rare.

passages.heading: 2.2. What This Pattern Reveals
passages.text: The selection itself is meaningful. The master did not randomly accumulate twenty teachers. He chose them based on his developing sense of what his path would be. The selection reflects judgment about what specializations matter, in what balance, for what purpose.

passages.heading: 2.2. What This Pattern Reveals
passages.text: The combination produces something new. What the master teaches is not the union of what his twenty teachers taught. It is something that emerges from the combination — informed by all twenty but not equivalent to their sum.

passages.heading: 2.2. What This Pattern Reveals
passages.text: The pattern is repeatable but not duplicable. Other students could similarly study with twenty teachers and develop their own composite mastery. But each composite would be different. The pattern of "study with many narrow specialists and synthesize" is general; the specific synthesis is individual.

passages.heading: 2.3. Why This Pattern Generalizes
passages.text: Music. Composers who studied with multiple teachers across genres, eras, instruments. Their compositional voice emerges from the combination, not from any single tradition.

passages.heading: 2.3. Why This Pattern Generalizes
passages.text: Medicine. Physicians who developed specific specialties combining multiple clinical areas — for example, oncology with focus on immunology and palliative care. Each component specialization is taught broadly; the combination is distinctive.

passages.heading: 2.3. Why This Pattern Generalizes
passages.text: Law. Lawyers who combine practice areas. A lawyer with expertise in technology law plus disability rights plus international human rights occupies a niche shaped by her specific combination, even though each area has many other practitioners.

passages.heading: 2.3. Why This Pattern Generalizes
passages.text: Software architecture. Engineers whose distinctive value comes from combining specialized skills — for example, systems performance engineering plus distributed systems plus security. Each skill is general; few engineers combine all three deeply.

passages.heading: 2.3. Why This Pattern Generalizes
passages.text: Academic research. Scholars whose work bridges fields. Computational biology, environmental humanities, science and technology studies — these fields exist because individuals combined specializations from multiple disciplines.

passages.heading: 2.3. Why This Pattern Generalizes
passages.text: Teaching. A teacher whose effectiveness comes from combining subject expertise mathematics , pedagogical approach constructivist , specialty working with neurodivergent students , and cultural competency multilingual classrooms . Each component is shared; the combination is rare.

passages.heading: 2.4. AI Architecture Should Mirror This
passages.text: If composite mastery is how skilled practitioners actually develop, AI infrastructure supporting them should make this pattern accessible — not just to those few who can find twenty human teachers, but to anyone who needs a particular combination of specializations.

passages.heading: 2.4. AI Architecture Should Mirror This
passages.text: The Composite Skills Agent makes this possible by maintaining a registry of narrow-specialist AI sub-agents that any practitioner can configure into their own combination.

passages.heading: 3. What Makes a Composite Skills Agent
passages.text: - 3. What Makes a Composite Skills Agent 3-what-makes-a-composite-skills-agent - 3.1. Defining Properties 31-defining-properties - 3.2. What a Composite Skills Agent Does 32-what-a-composite-skills-agent-does - 3.3. What a Composite Skills Agent Does NOT Do 33-what-a-composite-skills-agent-does-not-do - 3.4. The Twenty-Sub-Agents Constraint 34-the-twenty-sub-agents-constraint - 3.5. The Coordinato

passages.heading: 3.1. Defining Properties
passages.text: Property 1 — Configurable Sub-Agent Set. The composite agent draws on a specified set of narrow-specialist sub-agents, selected by the principal with possible AI assistance from a larger registry. The set is the configuration.

passages.heading: 3.1. Defining Properties
passages.text: Property 2 — Sub-Agents Are Individually General. Each sub-agent in the configuration is a Type 1-style Professional Colleague Agent for a narrow specialization, shared across all principals who need that specialization. Sub-agents are not built per-principal.

passages.heading: 3.1. Defining Properties
passages.text: Property 3 — Configuration Is Individually Specific. While each sub-agent is general, the specific combination belongs to the principal. A different principal would have a different configuration even within the same general profession.

passages.heading: 3.1. Defining Properties
passages.text: Property 4 — Coordinated Single-Surface Interaction. The principal interacts with the composite agent as a coherent whole, not with each sub-agent separately. The composite agent's job is to route, coordinate, and integrate.

passages.heading: 3.1. Defining Properties
passages.text: Property 5 — Transparent Source Attribution. When outputs draw on specific sub-agents, the principal can see which sub-agents contributed what. Attribution is preserved, not hidden.

passages.heading: 3.1. Defining Properties
passages.text: Property 6 — Disagreement Surfacing. When sub-agents disagree which is normal and informative , the composite agent surfaces the disagreement to the principal rather than masking it through averaging or arbitration.

passages.heading: 3.1. Defining Properties
passages.text: Property 7 — Configuration Evolution. The principal can add or remove sub-agents over time as their specializations develop. The configuration is not static.

passages.heading: 3.1. Defining Properties
passages.text: Property 8 — Principal Authorship Preserved. Despite drawing on many sub-agents, the principal remains the author of their work. Sub-agents inform; the principal decides and produces.

passages.heading: 3.2. What a Composite Skills Agent Does
passages.text: Function 1 — Routing. When the principal poses a question or task, the composite agent determines which sub-agents are relevant. Some questions need only one sub-agent. Others need several in combination. Some need a specific sequence one sub-agent's output feeds into another .

passages.heading: 3.2. What a Composite Skills Agent Does
passages.text: Function 2 — Synthesis. When multiple sub-agents contribute to a response, the composite agent integrates their outputs into a coherent whole — without obscuring which sub-agent contributed what.

passages.heading: 3.2. What a Composite Skills Agent Does
passages.text: Function 3 — Disagreement Management. When sub-agents provide conflicting recommendations, the composite agent presents the disagreement clearly to the principal: "Sub-agent A recommends X for these reasons; sub-agent B recommends Y for these reasons." The principal decides.

passages.heading: 3.2. What a Composite Skills Agent Does
passages.text: Function 4 — Configuration Maintenance. Over time, the composite agent helps the principal evaluate their configuration: which sub-agents are most used, which are underutilized, which specializations might be worth adding given the principal's work patterns.

passages.heading: 3.3. What a Composite Skills Agent Does NOT Do
passages.text: Not invent specializations. The composite agent does not create new sub-agents. It draws from the existing registry. Sub-agent creation is a separate process involving expert curation.

passages.heading: 3.3. What a Composite Skills Agent Does NOT Do
passages.text: Not replace principal judgment. When sub-agents disagree, the composite agent does not decide. The principal decides, informed by visible disagreement.

passages.heading: 3.3. What a Composite Skills Agent Does NOT Do
passages.text: Not hide its workings. The composite agent does not present synthesized outputs as if from a single source. Attribution is preserved.

passages.heading: 3.3. What a Composite Skills Agent Does NOT Do
passages.text: Not build permanent fixed configurations. The configuration is meant to evolve. The composite agent does not lock the principal into an unchangeable set.

passages.heading: 3.3. What a Composite Skills Agent Does NOT Do
passages.text: Not act externally. Like Professional Colleague Agents, the composite agent works inside the principal's professional practice. External representation is the role of Representative Agents Type 4 .

passages.heading: 3.4. The Twenty-Sub-Agents Constraint
passages.text: A configuration of two or three sub-agents is too narrow — it duplicates what specialized Professional Colleague Agents already do.

passages.heading: 3.4. The Twenty-Sub-Agents Constraint
passages.text: Empirically, complex skilled practice typically draws on between ten and thirty specializations. This range matches the cognitive bandwidth of human practitioners and the operational capacity of coordinating layers.

passages.heading: 3.4. The Twenty-Sub-Agents Constraint
passages.text: We suggest twenty as a reasonable working constraint: configurations under five are probably better served by Type 1; configurations over forty are probably indicating a need for narrower focus or different agent type.

passages.heading: 3.5. The Coordinator-Within-Configuration Pattern
passages.text: This means: - The coordinator does not have profession-specific expertise - The coordinator's expertise is in routing, synthesis, disagreement management - Different principals can use the same coordinator with different configurations - The coordinator is general; the configurations it manages are specific

passages.heading: 3.5. The Coordinator-Within-Configuration Pattern
passages.text: This is analogous to how an orchestra conductor is not another musician with their own instrument. The conductor's expertise is coordination of musicians, not playing music. Composite agents inherit this distinction.

passages.heading: 4. The Sub-Agent Registry
passages.text: - 4. The Sub-Agent Registry 4-the-sub-agent-registry - 4.1. What a Sub-Agent Is 41-what-a-sub-agent-is - 4.2. Registry Structure 42-registry-structure - 4.3. How Sub-Agents Get Built 43-how-sub-agents-get-built - 4.4. The Registry as Public Good 44-the-registry-as-public-good - 4.5. Curation and Trust 45-curation-and-trust

passages.heading: 4. The Sub-Agent Registry
passages.text: The infrastructure underlying Composite Skills Agents requires a curated registry of narrow-specialist sub-agents. This is a substantial architectural component deserving its own discussion.

passages.heading: 4.1. What a Sub-Agent Is
passages.text: A sub-agent in this context is functionally equivalent to a narrow Professional Colleague Agent Type 1 — but for a very narrow specialization.

passages.heading: 4.1. What a Sub-Agent Is
passages.text: Where a typical Professional Colleague Agent might serve "all teachers," a sub-agent might serve "all teachers working on constructivist pedagogy for mathematics in upper primary grades."

passages.heading: 4.1. What a Sub-Agent Is
passages.text: The narrowness is the point. Sub-agents are deeply specialized in their narrow areas. That depth is what makes the composite configuration valuable.

passages.heading: 4.2. Registry Structure
passages.text: Identity and scope. What does this sub-agent specialize in? What questions does it answer? What questions does it not answer?

passages.heading: 4.2. Registry Structure
passages.text: Boundary conditions. Where does this sub-agent's expertise end? What other specializations does it complement? What specializations does it not engage with?

passages.heading: 4.2. Registry Structure
passages.text: Quality indicators. How was the sub-agent's knowledge base curated? By whom? When last updated? What is the known error profile?

passages.heading: 4.3. How Sub-Agents Get Built
passages.text: Building a sub-agent is a substantial effort. Each requires: - Curated knowledge base for the narrow specialization - Quality assurance against expert practice - Integration interface compatible with composite agents - Documentation of scope, boundaries, and usage

passages.heading: 4.3. How Sub-Agents Get Built
passages.text: Model 1 — Expert-driven, paid. A curator with deep expertise in the specialization is paid to develop the sub-agent. Highest quality, slowest scaling.

passages.heading: 4.3. How Sub-Agents Get Built
passages.text: Model 2 — Expert-driven, voluntary. Practitioners contribute to sub-agent development as part of community participation. Lower direct cost, requires strong community.

passages.heading: 4.3. How Sub-Agents Get Built
passages.text: Model 3 — Federation contribution. Existing knowledge artifacts textbooks, methodological documents, case studies are integrated into sub-agent knowledge bases through partial automation plus expert review. Hybrid efficiency.

passages.heading: 4.3. How Sub-Agents Get Built
passages.text: Model 4 — Pattern library extraction. Patterns extracted from many practitioners' work anonymized through Nautilus-style anonymization pipeline become sub-agent training material. Highest scale, requires substantial active practice base.

passages.heading: 4.3. How Sub-Agents Get Built
passages.text: For mature ecosystems, all four models operate simultaneously. For new ecosystems like OKWF in early phases , Model 1 plus Model 3 are most feasible.

passages.heading: 4.4. The Registry as Public Good
passages.text: Arguments for open : prevents profession capture, allows multiple coordinators to compete, enables community contribution, matches mission of foundation-funded infrastructure.

passages.heading: 4.4. The Registry as Public Good
passages.text: For OKWF specifically, the open registry is strongly preferred. A proprietary registry concentrating control over which specializations exist would replicate exactly the kind of gatekeeping the foundation aims to dismantle.

passages.heading: 4.4. The Registry as Public Good
passages.text: The open registry model parallels how academic literature, open-source software libraries, and Creative Commons content work: many contributors, public access, multiple aggregators on top.

passages.heading: 4.5. Curation and Trust
passages.text: An open registry needs curation to maintain quality. Without curation, sub-agents of varying quality flood the registry, making coordination unreliable.

passages.heading: 4.5. Curation and Trust
passages.text: Curation can be: - Centralized : foundation-employed curators evaluate submissions - Distributed : peer review by qualified practitioners - Reputation-based : established sub-agents gain reputation through use; new ones earn reputation over time - Federated : multiple curating organizations operate, each with their own standards; principals choose which curators they trust

passages.heading: 4.5. Curation and Trust
passages.text: A combination is likely best. Foundation curators provide baseline; peer review distributes load; reputation accumulates over time; federation allows diverse views.

passages.heading: 4.5. Curation and Trust
passages.text: This is similar to how scholarly publishing works: journals provide gatekeeping, but the journal landscape includes diverse publishers with different standards, and reputation accumulates across both journals and individual researchers.

passages.heading: 5. Configuration: How Principals Build Their Ensembles
passages.text: - 5. Configuration: How Principals Build Their Ensembles 5-configuration-how-principals-build-their-ensembles - 5.1. The Configuration Problem 51-the-configuration-problem - 5.2. Three Configuration Approaches 52-three-configuration-approaches - 5.3. Hybrid Configuration 53-hybrid-configuration - 5.4. Configuration Evolution 54-configuration-evolution - 5.5. Templates and Starting Points 55-templa

passages.heading: 5.1. The Configuration Problem
passages.text: A principal entering an ecosystem with hundreds of available sub-agents faces a configuration problem. They do not yet know: - Which specializations are most relevant to their work - Which combinations work well together - Which sub-agents are highest quality - How their configuration should evolve over time

passages.heading: 5.1. The Configuration Problem
passages.text: This is similar to how a new yoga student facing hundreds of possible teachers doesn't know which twenty to pursue. The configuration emerges from a process, not a single decision.

passages.heading: 5.2. Three Configuration Approaches
passages.text: Approach 1 — Self-directed. The principal browses the registry, selects sub-agents based on their self-assessment, and configures their composite agent.

passages.heading: 5.2. Three Configuration Approaches
passages.text: Approach 2 — AI-assisted recommendation. The composite agent or a separate recommendation system analyzes the principal's stated goals, work history, and preferences, then suggests an initial configuration. Principal modifies as needed.

passages.heading: 5.2. Three Configuration Approaches
passages.text: Weaknesses: Risk of recommendation bias. Algorithmic suggestions may converge toward popular configurations, reducing diversity. Principal may accept recommendations passively without learning the registry.

passages.heading: 5.2. Three Configuration Approaches
passages.text: Approach 3 — Mentor-guided. An experienced practitioner — ideally one who has developed their own composite practice — helps the principal think through their configuration.

passages.heading: 5.2. Three Configuration Approaches
passages.text: Strengths: Deep human judgment about what configurations work. Mentor knows what the principal's stated goals actually require. Builds relationship beyond the technology.

passages.heading: 5.3. Hybrid Configuration
passages.text: 1. Initial AI recommendation based on principal's stated profile generates a starting configuration 2. Self-directed exploration lets principal browse, adjust, add specializations they recognize as relevant 3. Mentor review optional provides experienced human perspective, especially for principals new to their field

passages.heading: 5.3. Hybrid Configuration
passages.text: This three-step process echoes how human professional development actually works: training programs provide initial frameworks, self-directed learning fills gaps, and mentors provide deep guidance.

passages.heading: 5.4. Configuration Evolution
passages.text: A principal's configuration is not static. Specializations change over time: - New specializations enter their work as careers evolve - Old specializations become less relevant as priorities shift - New sub-agents become available in the registry - Old sub-agents may be deprecated

passages.heading: 5.4. Configuration Evolution
passages.text: The composite agent should help the principal monitor configuration health: - Which sub-agents are most used? - Which are rarely consulted? - Are there new sub-agents that match the principal's recent work patterns?

passages.heading: 5.5. Templates and Starting Points
passages.text: For many professions, common configurations exist. A new social law lawyer might benefit from a starting template: - General SGB IX sub-agent - General SGB XII sub-agent - Procedural law sub-agent - BSG case law sub-agent - General drafting sub-agent

passages.heading: 5.5. Templates and Starting Points
passages.text: This template provides a reasonable starting configuration. The practitioner then specializes by adding sub-agents specific to their developing practice e.g., disability rights specifically, or social welfare for elderly clients, or working with mental health conditions .

passages.heading: 6. Coordination and Disagreement Resolution
passages.text: - 6. Coordination and Disagreement Resolution 6-coordination-and-disagreement-resolution - 6.1. Why Sub-Agents Disagree 61-why-sub-agents-disagree - 6.2. Surfacing Versus Resolving 62-surfacing-versus-resolving - 6.3. Presentation of Disagreement 63-presentation-of-disagreement - 6.4. When the Principal Cannot Decide 64-when-the-principal-cannot-decide - 6.5. Learning from Resolution 65-learning-f

passages.heading: 6.1. Why Sub-Agents Disagree
passages.text: Different specialization frames. A sub-agent on legal strategy may recommend X; a sub-agent on case management may recommend Y. Each is correct within its frame.

passages.heading: 6.1. Why Sub-Agents Disagree
passages.text: Different evidence weights. A sub-agent may weight recent case law heavily; another may weight long-established methodology more. Both are reasonable choices that produce different recommendations.

passages.heading: 6.1. Why Sub-Agents Disagree
passages.text: These disagreements often contain the most important information the principal needs. A composite agent that resolved them silently would hide what the principal most needs to see.

passages.heading: 6.2. Surfacing Versus Resolving
passages.text: This is critical because: - Algorithmic resolution would impose hidden judgment on the principal - Resolution loses information that the disagreement contains - The principal's authority over their work depends on access to underlying disagreement - Profession-level differences in approach should remain visible, not be smoothed away

passages.heading: 6.3. Presentation of Disagreement
passages.text: Pattern 1 — Side-by-side recommendation. "Sub-agent A recommends X with reasoning ... ; sub-agent B recommends Y with reasoning ... ." Principal sees both directly.

passages.heading: 6.3. Presentation of Disagreement
passages.text: Pattern 2 — Common ground first. "Both sub-agents agree that base position . They differ on specific issue : A says X; B says Y." Helps principal see what is settled and what is contested.

passages.heading: 6.3. Presentation of Disagreement
passages.text: Pattern 3 — Compatibility analysis. "A's recommendation may produce result X. B's recommendation may produce result Y. The choice depends on whether you prioritize Z." Helps principal see what the disagreement actually means for outcomes.

passages.heading: 6.3. Presentation of Disagreement
passages.text: Pattern 4 — Resolution suggestion without execution . "If you favor X, here's how A's recommendation can be implemented. If you favor Y, here's how B's." Principal still chooses; the agent helps with implementation of the chosen direction.

passages.heading: 6.3. Presentation of Disagreement
passages.text: What does not work: averaging, voting, "balanced" outputs that mask the underlying disagreement. These appear neutral but actually impose hidden judgment.

passages.heading: 6.4. When the Principal Cannot Decide
passages.text: Escalation to mentor. If the principal has access to a human mentor, the composite agent can structure the disagreement for mentor review.

passages.heading: 6.4. When the Principal Cannot Decide
passages.text: Risk-weighted default. If one recommendation is more conservative smaller commitment, easier reversal , the composite agent can suggest defaulting to that while gathering more information.

passages.heading: 6.4. When the Principal Cannot Decide
passages.text: Acknowledged uncertainty. Sometimes the right output is: "You face a genuine professional judgment call here. Here's what each sub-agent recommends; here's what's at stake; this is a moment where your accumulated experience matters."

passages.heading: 6.5. Learning from Resolution
passages.text: - Which sub-agents the principal tends to follow when there's disagreement - Which contexts predict which choice - Whether the principal's choices show evolving patterns

passages.heading: 6.5. Learning from Resolution
passages.text: This information should be available to the principal their own pattern of decisions over time but not used to hide future disagreements. The point is not to predict and pre-resolve; the point is to give the principal insight into their own practice.

passages.heading: 14. ADR-001: Federation over Merging
passages.text: Context: При построении системы knowledge management встаёт выбор: заставить все данные мигрировать в единую схему merge , либо сохранить разные native-форматы и переводить по необходимости federation .

passages.heading: 14. ADR-001: Federation over Merging
passages.text: Positive: - Low barrier to entry: существующие Repos подключаются без рефакторинга - Authorship preserved: каждый автор работает в своей модели - Multi-angle views: один концепт может существовать в трёх Repos с тремя разными углами одновременно

passages.heading: 14. ADR-001: Federation over Merging
passages.text: Negative: - Cross-repo queries дороже: требуется вызов всех адаптеров - Consensus нетривиален: string matching — аппроксимация, не formal mapping - Bridges — текстовые описания, не machine-executable

passages.heading: 14. ADR-001: Federation over Merging
passages.text: Alternatives rejected: - Unified RDF/OWL ontology: слишком высокий barrier to entry - Centralized database: нарушает принцип local autonomy - Schema-less dump: теряется семантика angles

passages.heading: 7. Economics of Combinatorial Replication
passages.text: - 7. Economics of Combinatorial Replication 7-economics-of-combinatorial-replication - 7.1. Cost Structure 71-cost-structure - 7.2. Combinatorial Value 72-combinatorial-value - 7.3. Investment Strategy 73-investment-strategy - 7.4. Funding Models for Sub-Agent Development 74-funding-models-for-sub-agent-development - 7.5. Sustainable Economics for the SGB Pilot 75-sustainable-economics-for-the-sgb

passages.heading: 7.1. Cost Structure
passages.text: Sub-agent development : substantial fixed cost per sub-agent. A high-quality narrow-specialist sub-agent might cost €50,000- €500,000 to build, depending on knowledge base depth.

passages.heading: 7.1. Cost Structure
passages.text: Coordinator operation : low marginal cost per principal. LLM API costs scale with use, but the coordinator itself doesn't require per-principal customization beyond configuration.

passages.heading: 7.1. Cost Structure
passages.text: This produces interesting unit economics. If an ecosystem has 500 sub-agents and 10,000 principals, the ratio of sub-agent fixed cost to total user value is highly favorable.

passages.heading: 7.2. Combinatorial Value
passages.text: The number of possible configurations grows combinatorially: - 100 sub-agents, choose 15: ~250 billion possible configurations - 500 sub-agents, choose 20: astronomical

passages.heading: 7.2. Combinatorial Value
passages.text: In practice, configurations cluster around common patterns. But even within any profession, there's room for substantial diversity. Each principal's configuration genuinely reflects their distinctive practice.

passages.heading: 7.2. Combinatorial Value
passages.text: The combinatorial value is the central economic insight: a relatively small fixed investment in sub-agent development unlocks an essentially unlimited space of unique principal configurations.

passages.heading: 7.3. Investment Strategy
passages.text: Phase 1 : Build a small set of broadly applicable sub-agents perhaps 20-50 covering core specializations of one profession . This requires substantial upfront investment but enables initial configurations.

passages.heading: 7.3. Investment Strategy
passages.text: Phase 2 : Expand to more specialized sub-agents based on observed configuration gaps. As principals use the system, their requests for specializations not yet available drive priority for new sub-agent development.

passages.heading: 7.3. Investment Strategy
passages.text: Phase 3 : Federation with external sub-agent providers. Once the registry framework is established, sub-agents from other organizations can be integrated, accelerating coverage.

passages.heading: 7.3. Investment Strategy
passages.text: Phase 4 : Cross-profession expansion. Sub-agents developed for one profession may apply to adjacent professions. Methodology sub-agents particularly cross professional boundaries.

passages.heading: 7.3. Investment Strategy
passages.text: This phasing parallels how academic libraries develop: starting with core texts, expanding to specialized works, federating with other libraries, and enabling cross-disciplinary access.

passages.heading: 7.4. Funding Models for Sub-Agent Development
passages.text: Hybrid. Most realistic for early-stage ecosystem. Foundation funds core infrastructure, sponsors fund priority sub-agents, practitioners contribute to specific narrow sub-agents through volunteer expert work.

passages.heading: 7.5. Sustainable Economics for the SGB Pilot
passages.text: Instead of building one large profession-wide agent for German social law, build: - 20-30 narrow sub-agents covering core SGB specializations - A coordinator that practitioners configure individually - Templates for common practice patterns general advocacy, disability rights, elderly care, mental health, child welfare

passages.heading: 7.5. Sustainable Economics for the SGB Pilot
passages.text: Estimated investment: - 20 sub-agents at €50K average: €1,000,000 - Coordinator development: €300,000 - First-year operations: €200,000 - Total Year 1: €1.5 million

passages.heading: 7.5. Sustainable Economics for the SGB Pilot
passages.text: This is higher than the original estimate €430K but produces substantially more capability. Practitioners get composite configurations matching their specific practice, not a generic SGB agent.

passages.heading: 7.5. Sustainable Economics for the SGB Pilot
passages.text: For 1,000 practitioners over three years, this comes to approximately €500/practitioner/year — feasible for foundation funding or modest practitioner contribution.

passages.heading: 8. Seven Domains of Application
passages.text: - 8. Seven Domains of Application 8-seven-domains-of-application - 8.1. Domain 1 — Music Education and Performance 81-domain-1-music-education-and-performance - 8.2. Domain 2 — Visual and Creative Arts 82-domain-2-visual-and-creative-arts - 8.3. Domain 3 — Specialized Legal Practice 83-domain-3-specialized-legal-practice - 8.4. Domain 4 — Medical Practice with Multiple Specialties 84-domain-4-medi

passages.heading: 8. Seven Domains of Application
passages.text: Composite Skills Agents apply naturally to professions where practice typically combines specializations. We identify seven domains where the pattern fits especially well.

passages.heading: 8.1. Domain 1 — Music Education and Performance
passages.text: Sub-agent specializations might include : Classical composition, jazz improvisation, electronic music production, specific instruments piano, violin, voice, etc. , genres folk traditions, contemporary, baroque , pedagogy children, adults, beginners, advanced , technical skills music theory, ear training, sight-reading , accompanying disciplines audio engineering, music history, ethnomusicology .

passages.heading: 8.1. Domain 1 — Music Education and Performance
passages.text: Typical configurations : A jazz pianist who teaches has different needs from a classical violinist, who has different needs from an electronic music producer. Each builds their own configuration from the available sub-agents.

passages.heading: 8.1. Domain 1 — Music Education and Performance
passages.text: Existing analog : This pattern is already embodied in how musicians actually train — studying with multiple teachers in different specialties — but no AI infrastructure currently supports it.

passages.heading: 8.2. Domain 2 — Visual and Creative Arts
passages.text: Sub-agent specializations might include : Drawing fundamentals, painting techniques, digital illustration, animation, sculpture, photography, color theory, composition, art history by period or movement , specific tools Adobe Creative Suite, Procreate, Blender , business skills gallery work, commission management, intellectual property .

passages.heading: 8.2. Domain 2 — Visual and Creative Arts
passages.text: Typical configurations : A digital illustrator who also does traditional sketching and runs a small business has very different needs from a fine arts painter focused on gallery representation.

passages.heading: 8.2. Domain 2 — Visual and Creative Arts
passages.text: Bridge to existing infrastructure : Many artists already combine multiple educational sources online courses, traditional training, peer learning . Composite agents formalize and augment this practice.

passages.heading: 8.3. Domain 3 — Specialized Legal Practice
passages.text: Sub-agent specializations might include : Specific area of law social, criminal, family, immigration , procedural expertise specific courts, jurisdictions , client population elderly, disabled, immigrants, children , language German, English, Russian, etc. , technical skills drafting, oral advocacy, negotiation , supporting fields medical understanding, psychological assessment .

passages.heading: 8.3. Domain 3 — Specialized Legal Practice
passages.text: Typical configurations : A lawyer specializing in disability rights for migrants speaks both German and the migrant's language and has medical understanding for assessing claims — a combination requiring multiple sub-agents.

passages.heading: 8.4. Domain 4 — Medical Practice with Multiple Specialties
passages.text: Sub-agent specializations might include : Specific organ systems, types of conditions acute, chronic, infectious, autoimmune , patient populations pediatric, geriatric, prenatal , specialized procedures, diagnostic interpretation radiology, lab work, pathology , specific subspecialties rheumatology, immunology, infectious diseases .

passages.heading: 8.4. Domain 4 — Medical Practice with Multiple Specialties
passages.text: Typical configurations : A primary care physician working in a rural clinic with elderly patients faces different challenges from a specialist working in tertiary care. Even within specialties, working with specific patient populations requires different configurations.

passages.heading: 8.4. Domain 4 — Medical Practice with Multiple Specialties
passages.text: Critical caveat : Medical configurations require especially rigorous quality assurance and explicit boundaries around what the composite agent can support.

passages.heading: 8.5. Domain 5 — Technical Architecture and Engineering
passages.text: Sub-agent specializations might include : Programming languages, architecture patterns, deployment platforms, security disciplines, performance engineering, testing approaches, specific application domains fintech, medtech, e-commerce, infrastructure , compliance regimes.

passages.heading: 8.5. Domain 5 — Technical Architecture and Engineering
passages.text: Typical configurations : An engineer specializing in high-performance distributed systems for medical applications needs specific combinations: distributed systems, performance engineering, plus medical compliance, plus security requirements specific to healthcare.

passages.heading: 8.5. Domain 5 — Technical Architecture and Engineering
passages.text: Bridge to existing tools : Many engineering teams already informally combine specialist resources Stack Overflow, language-specific documentation, specialist consultants . Composite agents formalize this.

passages.heading: 8.6. Domain 6 — Academic Research and Cross-Disciplinary Work
passages.text: Sub-agent specializations might include : Specific methodologies quantitative, qualitative, mixed methods , research traditions, statistical approaches, computational methods, writing conventions for specific journals or fields, literature search across databases, ethical frameworks.

passages.heading: 8.6. Domain 6 — Academic Research and Cross-Disciplinary Work
passages.text: Typical configurations : A computational biologist working on epidemiology with humanities co-authors faces unique configuration needs combining biology, computer science, epidemiological methods, and humanities perspectives.

passages.heading: 8.7. Domain 7 — Therapeutic Practice and Mental Health
passages.text: Sub-agent specializations might include : Therapeutic modalities CBT, somatic experiencing, narrative therapy, gestalt , specific conditions trauma, addiction, eating disorders, mood disorders , client populations children, adolescents, elderly, couples, groups , supporting frameworks neuroscience, attachment theory, family systems , cultural competencies, specific skills crisis intervention, grou

passages.heading: 8.7. Domain 7 — Therapeutic Practice and Mental Health
passages.text: Typical configurations : A therapist working with adolescents who have eating disorders in immigrant families requires specific combinations spanning therapeutic modalities, developmental psychology, eating disorder specifics, and cultural competency.

passages.heading: 8.7. Domain 7 — Therapeutic Practice and Mental Health
passages.text: Critical caveat : Mental health practice requires especially careful attention to where AI support is appropriate research, documentation, knowledge lookup versus where it is not clinical judgment, therapeutic relationship . Composite agents operate strictly in the support role.

passages.heading: 8.8. Pattern Across Domains
passages.text: What unites these domains: - Practice is deeply specialized, but specialization combines rather than replaces general profession competency - Practitioners typically span 10-30 distinct specializations - Each specialization is shared with other practitioners but configurations are individually distinctive - Existing professional development already follows this pattern; composite agents formalize

passages.heading: 8.8. Pattern Across Domains
passages.text: The pattern is general. We expect Composite Skills Agents to become standard infrastructure across most skilled professions within 5-10 years.

passages.heading: 9. Integration with OKWF Infrastructure
passages.text: - 9. Integration with OKWF Infrastructure 9-integration-with-okwf-infrastructure - 9.1. Connection to Pattern Library 91-connection-to-pattern-library - 9.2. Connection to Guild Structure 92-connection-to-guild-structure - 9.3. Connection to Double-Triangle Architecture 93-connection-to-double-triangle-architecture - 9.4. Connection to Representative Agents 94-connection-to-representative-agents -

passages.heading: 9.1. Connection to Pattern Library
passages.text: The Nautilus pattern library architecture public patterns, private instances, anonymization pipeline provides foundational support for the sub-agent registry. Patterns become source material for sub-agents. Anonymized practitioner contributions flow into pattern library, which informs sub-agent updates.

passages.heading: 9.1. Connection to Pattern Library
passages.text: The connection is not just analogical. The same infrastructure serves both: - Pattern library: knowledge fragments accessible to humans for review - Sub-agent registry: knowledge fragments operationalized into AI assistants

passages.heading: 9.2. Connection to Guild Structure
passages.text: OKWF's guild structure groups of practitioners by professional area provides natural organization for sub-agent development. Each guild can: - Identify priority sub-agents for their area - Contribute expert curation - Test sub-agents in actual practice - Maintain quality standards - Develop common configuration templates

passages.heading: 9.3. Connection to Double-Triangle Architecture
passages.text: In the Double-Triangle Architecture, each Node human practitioner has lower-triangle assistants supporting their work. Composite Skills Agents formalize what lower-triangle assistants typically contain: not one undifferentiated assistant but a coordinated configuration of narrow specialists.

passages.heading: 9.3. Connection to Double-Triangle Architecture
passages.text: The composite agent itself is the orchestrator of the lower triangle. The sub-agents in the configuration are the actual "lower triangle assistants" doing specialized work.

passages.heading: 9.4. Connection to Representative Agents
passages.text: Composite Skills Agents and Representative Agents work together naturally: - The composite agent supports the practitioner's work - The representative agent supports the practitioner's external position

passages.heading: 9.4. Connection to Representative Agents
passages.text: A mature practitioner has both: composite for daily work, representative for opportunity navigation. They share underlying profile information about the practitioner but operate in different layers.

passages.heading: 9.5. Refined OKWF Rollout Plan
passages.text: Phase 0 Months 1-12 : Foundation establishment, initial sub-agent set for one profession perhaps SGB social law . Build 20-30 narrow sub-agents plus coordinator. Pilot with 5-10 practitioners.

passages.heading: 9.5. Refined OKWF Rollout Plan
passages.text: Phase 1 Year 2 : Expand sub-agent set, scale practitioners to 100-500. Establish pattern library connection, anonymization pipeline. Begin contribution flow back into sub-agent updates.

passages.heading: 9.5. Refined OKWF Rollout Plan
passages.text: Phase 2 Years 2-3 : Expand to second profession perhaps educational support for disabled students, maintaining thematic connection . Cross-profession integration of methodology sub-agents.

passages.heading: 9.5. Refined OKWF Rollout Plan
passages.text: Phase 3 Years 3-5 : Multiple professions, federated sub-agent registry, mature configuration management. Begin adding Representative Agent layer for principals who want it.

passages.heading: 9.5. Refined OKWF Rollout Plan
passages.text: This plan is more realistic than the earlier proposal because it matches how skilled practitioners actually develop and what they actually need.

passages.heading: 9.6. Practical Implication for Pilot Design
passages.text: Cost increases €1.5M vs €430K for Year 1 , but produces a genuinely useful infrastructure that scales to multiple adjacent legal domains and supports practitioners' actual diverse practices rather than forcing one-size-fits-all generalization.

passages.heading: 9.6. Practical Implication for Pilot Design
passages.text: This is a better proposal for foundation funders even at higher cost, because it demonstrates a more sophisticated understanding of how skilled practice works and produces infrastructure with broader applicability.

passages.heading: 10. Risks Specific to Composite Architectures
passages.text: - 10. Risks Specific to Composite Architectures 10-risks-specific-to-composite-architectures - 10.1. Risk: Configuration Paralysis 101-risk-configuration-paralysis - 10.2. Risk: Configuration Echo Chamber 102-risk-configuration-echo-chamber - 10.3. Risk: Sub-Agent Dependency 103-risk-sub-agent-dependency - 10.4. Risk: Coordinator Bias 104-risk-coordinator-bias - 10.5. Risk: Quality Variation Acros

passages.heading: 10.1. Risk: Configuration Paralysis
passages.text: Scenario : Faced with hundreds of available sub-agents, the principal cannot decide which to include. Configuration becomes overwhelming task. Principal gives up and uses generic Professional Colleague Agent instead, missing the value of composite.

passages.heading: 10.1. Risk: Configuration Paralysis
passages.text: Mitigations : - Strong starting templates for common professional patterns - AI-assisted recommendation as default with override - Limit on initial configuration size start with 5-10, grow over time - Mentor connection for complex configuration decisions

passages.heading: 10.2. Risk: Configuration Echo Chamber
passages.text: Scenario : Principal selects sub-agents that confirm their existing approach, missing specializations that would challenge or expand their practice. Composite becomes a sophisticated form of confirmation bias.

passages.heading: 10.2. Risk: Configuration Echo Chamber
passages.text: Mitigations : - Recommendation system explicitly suggests "growth-edge" sub-agents outside principal's current configuration - Regular configuration reviews with prompts to consider unfamiliar specializations - Mentor input that intentionally surfaces alternative perspectives - Public visibility of how others in the same profession configure differently

passages.heading: 10.3. Risk: Sub-Agent Dependency
passages.text: Scenario : Principal becomes dependent on a specific sub-agent that is later deprecated or significantly changed. Their practice disrupts when configuration needs to change.

passages.heading: 10.3. Risk: Sub-Agent Dependency
passages.text: Mitigations : - Open standards make sub-agents portable and replaceable - Multiple sub-agents covering same specialization compete on quality, not lock-in - Notification when sub-agent will be deprecated, with migration paths - Configuration export so principal can move between coordinator providers

passages.heading: 10.4. Risk: Coordinator Bias
passages.text: Scenario : The coordinator general infrastructure managing configurations develops biases in routing or synthesis that systematically favor some sub-agents over others. Practice patterns shift in ways principals didn't choose.

passages.heading: 10.4. Risk: Coordinator Bias
passages.text: Mitigations : - Open coordinator architecture, with multiple coordinator options - Transparent routing logic principal can see why sub-agent was selected - Auditable behavior over time - Coordinator evaluation as part of platform governance

passages.heading: 10.5. Risk: Quality Variation Across Sub-Agents
passages.text: Scenario : Sub-agent registry includes high-quality and low-quality sub-agents. Principals using lower-quality sub-agents in their configurations get poorer support without realizing it.

passages.heading: 10.5. Risk: Quality Variation Across Sub-Agents
passages.text: Mitigations : - Visible quality indicators on sub-agents - Curation standards for inclusion in main registry - User feedback loops affecting visibility - Federated curation allowing principals to choose which curators they trust

passages.heading: 10.6. Risk: Profession Fragmentation
passages.text: Scenario : As composite configurations diversify, professional practice fragments. Practitioners working on similar problems develop incompatible approaches because they used different sub-agent configurations. Professional coherence weakens.

passages.heading: 10.6. Risk: Profession Fragmentation
passages.text: Mitigations : - Sub-agents share common foundational knowledge so configurations diverge from a common base - Guild structure maintains profession-level community across diverse configurations - Cross-configuration patterns in shared pattern library - Recognition that some fragmentation is healthy diversification versus unhealthy disconnection

passages.heading: 10.7. Risk: Hidden Dependencies Among Sub-Agents
passages.text: Scenario : A configuration appears to work well, but its output depends on subtle interactions between specific sub-agents. When one sub-agent updates, the configuration's output changes in ways neither principal nor coordinator anticipated.

passages.heading: 10.7. Risk: Hidden Dependencies Among Sub-Agents
passages.text: Mitigations : - Version pinning configurations specify exact sub-agent versions, opt into updates - Compatibility testing when sub-agents update - Notification of significant behavioral changes in sub-agents - Configuration testing against past examples to detect drift

passages.heading: 10.8. Risk: Configuration as Competitive Advantage Hoarding
passages.text: Scenario : Principals discover that specific configurations are particularly effective. They keep these configurations secret as competitive advantage. Configuration knowledge becomes hoarded rather than shared, limiting benefit to broader practice.

passages.heading: 10.8. Risk: Configuration as Competitive Advantage Hoarding
passages.text: Mitigations : - Default openness about configurations shared by default, opt-in to keep private - Anonymized configuration patterns published in pattern library - Recognition for sharing effective configurations - Cooperative ownership models that align individual benefit with collective improvement

passages.heading: 11.1. Granularity Questions
passages.text: How narrow should sub-agents be? Too narrow and configurations become unmanageable; too broad and the composite pattern collapses back to Professional Colleague Agent.

passages.heading: 11.1. Granularity Questions
passages.text: What's the right granularity for different professions? Likely varies — music might support finer granularity than law. Empirical question requiring deployment.

passages.heading: 11.2. Configuration Stability
passages.text: How often should principals revise configurations? Too often and they spend more time configuring than working; too rarely and configurations drift from current practice.

passages.heading: 11.2. Configuration Stability
passages.text: What signals suggest configuration revision is needed? Likely some combination of: significant change in principal's work, new sub-agents available, observed gaps in current configuration, explicit annual review.

passages.heading: 11.3. Cross-Professional Configurations
passages.text: When practitioners genuinely span professions lawyer who is also social worker, doctor who is also researcher , can their configuration span sub-agents from multiple professions? How does the coordinator handle this?

passages.heading: 11.4. Configuration Evolution at Scale
passages.text: If 10,000 practitioners use composite agents, what happens across the population? Do configurations cluster around patterns? Does this clustering match natural sub-specialties or create artificial groupings?

passages.heading: 11.5. Sub-Agent Quality Across Cultures
passages.text: Sub-agents developed primarily in one cultural or linguistic context may not serve well in others. How does the registry handle cultural variation? Multiple parallel sub-agents for different contexts? Configurable cultural overlays?

passages.heading: 11.6. Liability for Composite Outputs
passages.text: When a composite agent's output drawing on multiple sub-agents contains errors, who bears responsibility? The principal who used it? The coordinator? Each sub-agent that contributed? The platform?

passages.heading: 11.7. The "Emergence" Question
passages.text: When many sub-agents combine in a configuration, do useful properties emerge that weren't present in any individual sub-agent? Or do composite outputs simply average their contributions?

passages.heading: 11.8. Transition from Composite to Representative
passages.text: As principals develop sophisticated composite configurations, when if ever does it make sense to graduate to a Representative Agent Type 4 with truly individual specialization? Or do composites serve indefinitely?

passages.heading: 12.1. For Practitioners
passages.text: Tell us how your practice combines specializations. What configuration of sub-agents would actually serve your work? What would make composite agents valuable versus merely complex?

passages.heading: 12.2. For Curators and Domain Experts
passages.text: Sub-agent registry development needs curated knowledge across many narrow specializations. Domain experts willing to help develop sub-agents in their specialties — particularly compensated through foundation funding — would substantially accelerate deployment.

passages.heading: 12.3. For Software Engineers and Architects
passages.text: Coordinator infrastructure is substantial engineering work. Open-source contributions to reference implementations welcome. Particular needs: - Sub-agent registry standards and protocols - Configuration management interfaces - Disagreement detection and presentation systems - Audit and observability tools

passages.heading: 12.4. For Researchers
passages.text: Empirical studies of composite configuration patterns would illuminate questions raised in Section 11. Particular value in: - Configuration clustering analysis - Effectiveness comparison: composite vs. monolithic agents - Long-term effects on professional development - Cross-cultural deployment outcomes

passages.heading: 12.5. For Foundation Funders
passages.text: Composite Skills Agents represent higher-leverage infrastructure investment than monolithic Professional Colleague Agents: - More flexible support across diverse practitioner needs - More scalable across adjacent professions - More aligned with how mastery actually develops - More resilient to changes in any single component

passages.heading: 12.6. For Policymakers
passages.text: Regulatory frameworks for AI in professional practice will need to address composite architectures specifically. Questions about liability, professional responsibility, and quality standards apply differently when many specialized sub-agents contribute to outputs.

passages.heading: 13. Closing
passages.text: Mastery in skilled work has always been composite. The yoga master who studied with twenty teachers did not invent his practice from nothing; he combined what he learned from specialists. The musician, the lawyer, the physician, the architect, the therapist — all develop their distinctive practice by combining individually general skills into individually distinctive configurations.

passages.heading: 13. Closing
passages.text: What is new is the possibility of AI infrastructure that mirrors this structure. Until now, AI support for skilled work has been either too generic one assistant for everyone or too custom unique development per principal . Neither matches how practitioners actually develop and operate.

passages.heading: 13. Closing
passages.text: Composite Skills Agents fill this gap. They make the twenty-teacher pattern accessible at scale: any practitioner can configure their own ensemble of specialist sub-agents, creating support that matches their actual practice rather than forcing them into pre-defined categories.

passages.heading: 13. Closing
passages.text: This recognition changes the practical strategy for AI deployment in professions. Building monolithic profession-wide agents serves narrow purposes well but cannot serve the more common case of practitioners with distinctive specialized configurations. Building uniquely customized agents serves deep representational needs but cannot scale.

passages.heading: 13. Closing
passages.text: Building composite infrastructure — registry of narrow sub-agents plus configurable coordinators — serves the broad middle where most skilled practice actually happens.

passages.heading: 13. Closing
passages.text: For the Open Knowledge Work Foundation specifically, composite infrastructure provides: - More flexible support for diverse practitioner needs - Stronger alignment with mission of empowering distinctive practitioners - Better economic structure combinatorial leverage of fixed investment - More natural connection to existing pattern library architecture - Clearer migration path from initial pilot t

passages.heading: 13. Closing
passages.text: The question is whether we build it carefully — with open standards, foundation governance, quality curation, and practitioner ownership — or whether we let it emerge through commercial dynamics that favor concentration over distribution.

passages.heading: Acknowledgments
passages.text: The Composite Skills Agent concept emerged from dialogue with Claude Anthropic on April 26, 2026. The crystallizing contribution was the user's observation that the binary view between Professional Colleague Agent Type 1 and Representative Agent Type 4 missed the most common middle case — practitioners whose distinctive value comes from combining individually general specializations.

passages.heading: Acknowledgments
passages.text: The metaphor of the Indian yoga master who studied with twenty teachers, drawn from a journalist's interview the user remembered, provides the central illustration that makes the pattern clear.

passages.heading: Acknowledgments

passages.heading: Acknowledgments
passages.text: Together these papers form an architectural vision for AI-supported knowledge work that respects how mastery develops, how professions function, and how human dignity is preserved when AI augments human practice.

passages.heading: Appendix B: Sub-Agent Registry Schema (Sketch)
passages.text: This schema captures what coordinators need to route effectively, what principals need to evaluate fit, and what curators need to maintain quality.

passages.heading: Appendix C: Configuration Template Example
passages.text: This template provides a starting point that practitioners modify based on their actual practice. Some will add sub-agents for additional specializations. Some will remove sub-agents they don't need. Each ends up with a configuration distinctive to their practice.

passages.heading: Appendix C: Configuration Template Example
passages.text: The "optional growth edges" section deliberately suggests specializations the practitioner might add as their practice develops, encouraging exploration beyond the initial configuration.
